food_subgroup: Braised or stewed fish dishes with vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for clay pot fish with cabbage; closest parent 'fish dish' (FOODON_03302051) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and fish products - cooked fish dishes
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (cooked fish dishes) used as closest parent; no specific code for clay pot fish with cabbage exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific clay pot fish and cabbage dish.

main_ingredients: sliced fish (typically white fish such as batang or snapper)

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of cabbage and sliced fish clay pot is approximately 400 g, comprising a generous portion of braised cabbage, sliced white fish, and broth served in a clay pot.

nutrient_source.title: Fish and Vegetable Soup / Braised Fish with Vegetables — composite estimate derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for steamed/braised fish dishes and cooked cabbage, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked white fish (cod/snapper) and cooked cabbage
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for 'Cabbage and Sliced Fish Clay Pot' as a single dish. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of HPB Foodo data for braised/steamed fish dishes (~60% fish, ~30% cabbage, ~10% sauce/seasoning by weight) and USDA SR Legacy data for cooked white fish (cod, USDA #15016) and cooked cabbage (USDA #11110). Sodium is elevated to reflect soy sauce and fish sauce seasoning typical of this dish. All values represent a plausible per-100 g estimate for the mixed dish as served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed fish and vegetable dishes — analogous estimate based on GI values for cooked white fish (~0, negligible carbohydrate contribution) and cooked cabbage (GI ~10, low-GI vegetable), with soy-sauce-based broth contributing minimal glycemic load; composite dish GI estimated at ~32 consistent with low-GI mixed fish-vegetable dishes reported in the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cabbage and sliced fish clay pot. GI estimated at 32 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database reference values for cooked fish (GI effectively 0 due to negligible carbohydrate) and cooked cabbage (GI ~10). The small carbohydrate contribution (~3.8 g/100 g) comes primarily from cabbage and trace sauce ingredients. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (32 × 15.2 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.9 g, confirming very low glycemic impact. This estimate is consistent with published GI data for comparable low-carbohydrate fish and vegetable dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and fish sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 400 g serving of this dish may contribute approximately 1,680 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce preparation or limiting portion size is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 32 and only 3.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, this dish has a very low glycemic load and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean white fish protein with low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The lean white fish provides high-quality protein with low saturated fat (0.5 g/100 g) and modest polyunsaturated fatty acids; however, the elevated sodium from seasoning warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease, and reduced-sodium preparation is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cabbage and Sliced Fish Clay Pot (白菜鱼片煲, Bai Cai Yu Pian Bao) is a traditional Chinese braised dish popular in Singapore and southern China. It is prepared by simmering sliced white fish — typically batang (Spanish mackerel), snapper, or threadfin — together with napa or white cabbage in a seasoned broth inside a clay pot. The clay pot retains heat evenly and imparts a subtle earthen quality to the dish. The broth is typically seasoned with light soy sauce, fish sauce, ginger, garlic, sesame oil, and white pepper, producing a savoury, mildly umami flavour profile. The fish is tender and delicate, while the cabbage becomes soft and absorbs the broth flavours. This dish is commonly served as part of a shared Chinese meal alongside steamed rice and other dishes. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with lean fish providing approximately 8.5 g protein per 100 g and only 2.2 g fat, making it a relatively light and nutritious option. The glycemic index is low (estimated ~32), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose levels. The primary clinical concern is sodium content from soy sauce and fish sauce, which can be significant in restaurant preparations. Cabbage contributes dietary fibre, vitamin C, and folate, while the fish provides vitamin B12, niacin, and phosphorus. This dish is halal-friendly and dairy-free, and is widely available at Chinese zi char restaurants and hawker centres in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact match for 'Cabbage and Sliced Fish Clay Pot' was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any other primary approved source as a single composite dish entry. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of HPB Foodo braised/steamed fish dish data and USDA SR Legacy data for cooked white fish and cooked cabbage. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database reference values for component foods. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. Values should be treated as plausible approximations pending a direct laboratory analysis or an exact HPB Foodo match. 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the composite source entries used; white fish does contain some vitamin D but the mixed-dish value cannot be confidently assigned without a direct measurement. 3. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary composite sources used for this estimation. 4. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate. 5. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/zero for this type of minimally processed fish and vegetable dish per USDA SR Legacy data — confirmed zero. 6. GI is an analogous estimate (approximately 32, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database reference values for cooked white fish and cooked cabbage; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 7. Sodium is elevated (~420 mg/100 g) due to soy sauce and fish sauce seasoning typical of restaurant preparation; home-prepared versions may have significantly lower sodium. 8. The field dataed in the source data (Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles) is a Lanzhou-style noodle restaurant; the presence of this dish on their menu suggests it may be a supplementary offering. Nutrient values are based on the generic dish profile rather than this specific restaurant's recipe.
